nativeRange.habitat: The Opae Ula is endemic to the anchialine pools of the Hawaiian Islands — landlocked pools in porous lava or limestone rock that have no surface connection to the sea but are linked to it underground, which gives them a fluctuating, low-end brackish salinity. The shrimp grazes the algal and bacterial mats coating the rock surfaces of these pools and retreats into the rock's crevices, which is why porous rock is central to how it is kept.

decorPreferences: porous lava rock or coral rubble for grazing and shelter
aragonite or crushed-coral substrate to buffer a high, stable pH
macroalgae such as Chaetomorpha for cover and grazing
a tightly-fitting lid to slow evaporation and prevent escapes

diet.notes: The Opae Ula is a grazer that feeds on the film of algae and bacteria (biofilm) coating rocks, glass, and substrate, along with detritus. In a mature setup it needs very little supplemental food; a small amount of powdered algae, spirulina, or a purpose-made Opae Ula food every week or two is generally sufficient, and well-established colonies can go for extended periods on biofilm alone. Overfeeding is a more common problem than underfeeding.

avoidWith: fish and other predators, which will eat these tiny shrimp
larger or faster shrimp that outcompete them for biofilm
any tankmate needing freshwater, full marine salinity, or regular water changes

imageCandidates.notes: Plantlady223, 1200x900. Tack-sharp whole shrimp against a clean pale background showing the vivid brick-red coloration; no overlay, best hero shot.
Carnat Joel, ~1024x595. Sharp, vibrant red pair of Opae Ula grazing over crushed-coral substrate with a snail; conveys the colonial aquarium look; minor thin caption banner across the top.
Steph Brulot-Sawchyn, 1423x1897. Two intensely red shrimp on dark aquarium substrate; good colour and colonial context, main subject reasonably crisp.

summary: The Opae Ula (Halocaridina rubra) is a tiny brackish-water shrimp, rarely longer than 1.5 cm, endemic to the anchialine pools of Hawaii. It is widely regarded as one of the hardiest shrimp in the hobby: it tolerates a broad range of conditions, needs no filter or heater, and can live well over a decade in a small, stable, self-sustaining colony.
careNotes: The Opae Ula is generally considered one of the most forgiving invertebrates available to aquarists, which is why it is so often sold for sealed jar 'ecospheres'. It is kept in low-end brackish water — a mix of fresh and salt water — at a specific gravity of roughly 1.008 to 1.018, with many keepers settling on the more stable 1.010 to 1.012. The water is usually held hard and alkaline, around pH 8.0 to 8.5, and a calcium-based substrate such as aragonite or crushed coral helps keep the pH high without ongoing adjustment. Unusually, the species needs no filter, heater, or routine water changes; it does well at room temperature, and only evaporated water needs topping off, using fresh (not salt) water so that the salinity stays steady. What it does need is stability, as sudden swings in salinity are among the few things that reliably stress it. A little light encourages the algae and biofilm it grazes, and supplemental feeding is minimal. Like all invertebrates it is highly sensitive to copper, so copper-based medications should be avoided. Opae Ula are colonial and tend to be bolder and to colour up better in a group, so they are usually kept in numbers — often ten or more — and tolerate surprisingly high stocking densities. Almost everything about the species is slow: it grows slowly, moults infrequently, and can live well beyond a decade, so patience is the main thing it asks of a keeper.
breedingNotes: Unlike many aquarium shrimp, the Opae Ula completes its whole life cycle in the same brackish water it lives in, so an established colony tends to reproduce slowly on its own without any special rearing setup. Females carry only a small number of eggs and do not become gravid often, and the young pass through several free-swimming larval stages before settling as miniature shrimp. Growth is very slow, with animals generally taking around a year to reach maturity, so a colony builds up gradually over months and years rather than in obvious bursts.

additional.name: Windy City Aquariums
additional.notes: Secondary cross-check. Agreed on salinity SG 1.008–1.018 (ideal 1.010–1.012) and anchialine-pool origin; gave lifespan 10–15 years (up to 20) and a lower tank-size floor (1–5 gal, at least 2 recommended) than the primary's 5-gallon recommendation. Its adult size 'up to 1 inch' runs high against Wikipedia and the primary; the conservative 1.0–1.5 cm was used.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Halocaridina rubra
additional.notes: Taxonomy and biology cross-check. Confirmed family Atyidae, order Decapoda, adult length 'rarely longer than 1.5 cm', endemic Hawaiian anchialine-pool habitat, and a herbivorous/detritivorous diet of algal and bacterial mats.

additional.name: The Shrimp Farm
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 on access and could not be cross-checked; listed for traceability. Windy City Aquariums and Wikipedia were used as the substitute secondary and taxonomy sources.

crustacean.moltingNotes: In keeping with its very slow metabolism, the Opae Ula moults infrequently, and reliable interval figures are not well documented. Shed exoskeletons can be left in the tank as a calcium source. Because the species is adapted to extremely stable water, large or sudden changes in salinity are best avoided, as they are more likely to cause trouble than its otherwise undemanding care would suggest.
crustacean.breedingNotes: The Opae Ula is one of the few shrimp whose larvae develop in place rather than needing to migrate to a different salinity, which is why colonies can be self-sustaining in a simple jar or nano tank. Reported development passes through several zoeal stages and a mysis stage before the young metamorphose into juveniles, and animals generally take about a year to reach breeding age. Reproduction tends to be slow and low in yield, so a population grows gradually.
